drain()

    # Now all of the CPUs are ready to be switched out
    for old_cpu, new_cpu in cpuList:
        old_cpu.switchOut()

    # Change the memory mode if required. We check if this is needed
    # to avoid printing a warning if no switch was performed.
    if system.getMemoryMode() != memory_mode:
        # Flush the memory system if we are switching to a memory mode
        # that disables caches. This typically happens when switching to a
        # hardware virtualized CPU.
        if memory_mode == MemoryMode("atomic_noncaching").getValue():
            memWriteback(system)
            memInvalidate(system)

        _changeMemoryMode(system, memory_mode)

    for old_cpu, new_cpu in cpuList:
        new_cpu.takeOverFrom(old_cpu)


def notifyFork(root):
    for obj in root.descendants():
        obj.notifyFork()

    sequence number. The name of the output directory can be
    overridden using the simout keyword argument.

44	class SwitchableProcessor(AbstractProcessor):
45	    """
46	    This class can be used to setup a switchable processor/processors on a
47	    system using SimpleCores.
48	
49	    Though this class can be used directly, it is best inherited from. See
50	    SimpleSwitchableCPU for an example of this.
51	    """
52	
53	    def __init__(
54	        self,
55	        switchable_cores: Dict[str, List[SimpleCore]],
56	        starting_cores: str,
57	    ) -> None:
58	        if starting_cores not in switchable_cores.keys():
59	            raise AssertionError(
60	                f"Key {starting_cores} cannot be found in the "
61	                "switchable_processors dictionary."
62	            )
63	
64	        self._current_cores = switchable_cores[starting_cores]
65	        self._switchable_cores = switchable_cores
66	
67	        # In the stdlib we assume the system processor conforms to a single
68	        # ISA target.
69	        assert len({core.get_isa() for core in self._current_cores}) == 1
70	        super().__init__(isa=self._current_cores[0].get_isa())
71	
72	        for name, core_list in self._switchable_cores.items():
73	            # Use the names from the user as the member variables
74	            # This makes the stats print more nicely.
75	            setattr(self, name, core_list)
76	            for core in core_list:
77	                core.set_switched_out(core not in self._current_cores)
78	
79	        self._prepare_kvm = any(
80	            core.is_kvm_core() for core in self._all_cores()
81	        )
82	
83	        if self._prepare_kvm:
84	            from m5.objects import KvmVM
85	
86	            self.kvm_vm = KvmVM()
87	
88	    @overrides(AbstractProcessor)
89	    def incorporate_processor(self, board: AbstractBoard) -> None:
90	        # This is a bit of a hack. The `m5.switchCpus` function, used in the
91	        # "switch_to_processor" function, requires the System simobject as an
92	        # argument. We therefore need to store the board when incorporating the
93	        # procsesor
94	        self._board = board
95	
96	        if self._prepare_kvm:
97	            # To get the KVM CPUs to run on different host CPUs
98	            # Specify a different event queue for each CPU
99	            kvm_cores = [
100	                core for core in self._all_cores() if core.is_kvm_core()
101	            ]
102	            for i, core in enumerate(kvm_cores):
103	                for obj in core.get_simobject().descendants():
104	                    obj.eventq_index = 0
105	                core.get_simobject().eventq_index = i + 1
106	
107	    @overrides(AbstractProcessor)
108	    def get_num_cores(self) -> int:
109	        # Note: This is a special case where the total number of cores in the
110	        # design is not the number of cores, due to some being switched out.
111	        return len(self._current_cores)
112	
113	    @overrides(AbstractProcessor)
114	    def get_cores(self) -> List[AbstractCore]:
115	        return self._current_cores
116	
117	    def _all_cores(self):
118	        for core_list in self._switchable_cores.values():
119	            yield from core_list
120	
121	    def switch_to_processor(self, switchable_core_key: str):
122	        # Run various checks.
123	        if not hasattr(self, "_board"):
124	            raise AssertionError(
125	                "The processor has not been incorporated. "
126	                "If you are using multisim, please use "
127	                "simulator.switch_processor() instead of processor.switch()."
128	            )
129	
130	        if switchable_core_key not in self._switchable_cores.keys():
131	            raise AssertionError(
132	                f"Key {switchable_core_key} is not a key in the"
133	                " switchable_processor dictionary."
134	            )
135	
136	        # Select the correct processor to switch to.
137	        to_switch = self._switchable_cores[switchable_core_key]
138	
139	        # Run more checks.
140	        if to_switch == self._current_cores:
141	            raise AssertionError(
142	                "Cannot swap current cores with the current cores"
143	            )
144	
145	        if len(to_switch) != len(self._current_cores):
146	            raise AssertionError(
147	                "The number of cores to swap in is not the same as the number "
148	                "already swapped in. This is not allowed."
149	            )

from typing import (
    Dict,
    List,
)

import m5
from m5.objects import Root

from ...utils.override import *
from ..boards.abstract_board import AbstractBoard
from .abstract_core import AbstractCore
from .abstract_processor import AbstractProcessor
from .cpu_types import CPUTypes
from .simple_core import SimpleCore


class SwitchableProcessor(AbstractProcessor):
    """
    This class can be used to setup a switchable processor/processors on a
    system using SimpleCores.

    Though this class can be used directly, it is best inherited from. See
    SimpleSwitchableCPU for an example of this.
    """

    def __init__(
        self,
        switchable_cores: Dict[str, List[SimpleCore]],
        starting_cores: str,
    ) -> None:
        if starting_cores not in switchable_cores.keys():
            raise AssertionError(
                f"Key {starting_cores} cannot be found in the "
                "switchable_processors dictionary."
            )

        self._current_cores = switchable_cores[starting_cores]
        self._switchable_cores = switchable_cores

        # In the stdlib we assume the system processor conforms to a single
        # ISA target.
        assert len({core.get_isa() for core in self._current_cores}) == 1
        super().__init__(isa=self._current_cores[0].get_isa())

        for name, core_list in self._switchable_cores.items():
            # Use the names from the user as the member variables
            # This makes the stats print more nicely.
            setattr(self, name, core_list)
            for core in core_list:
                core.set_switched_out(core not in self._current_cores)

        self._prepare_kvm = any(
            core.is_kvm_core() for core in self._all_cores()
        )

        if self._prepare_kvm:
            from m5.objects import KvmVM

            self.kvm_vm = KvmVM()

    @overrides(AbstractProcessor)
    def incorporate_processor(self, board: AbstractBoard) -> None:
        # This is a bit of a hack. The `m5.switchCpus` function, used in the
        # "switch_to_processor" function, requires the System simobject as an
        # argument. We therefore need to store the board when incorporating the
        # procsesor
        self._board = board

        if self._prepare_kvm:
            # To get the KVM CPUs to run on different host CPUs
            # Specify a different event queue for each CPU
            kvm_cores = [
                core for core in self._all_cores() if core.is_kvm_core()
            ]
            for i, core in enumerate(kvm_cores):
                for obj in core.get_simobject().descendants():
                    obj.eventq_index = 0
                core.get_simobject().eventq_index = i + 1

    @overrides(AbstractProcessor)
    def get_num_cores(self) -> int:
        # Note: This is a special case where the total number of cores in the
        # design is not the number of cores, due to some being switched out.
        return len(self._current_cores)

    @overrides(AbstractProcessor)
    def get_cores(self) -> List[AbstractCore]:
        return self._current_cores

    def _all_cores(self):
        for core_list in self._switchable_cores.values():
            yield from core_list

    def switch_to_processor(self, switchable_core_key: str):
        # Run various checks.
        if not hasattr(self, "_board"):
            raise AssertionError(
                "The processor has not been incorporated. "
                "If you are using multisim, please use "
                "simulator.switch_processor() instead of processor.switch()."
            )

        if switchable_core_key not in self._switchable_cores.keys():
            raise AssertionError(
                f"Key {switchable_core_key} is not a key in the"
                " switchable_processor dictionary."
            )

        # Select the correct processor to switch to.
        to_switch = self._switchable_cores[switchable_core_key]

        # Run more checks.
        if to_switch == self._current_cores:
            raise AssertionError(
                "Cannot swap current cores with the current cores"
            )

        if len(to_switch) != len(self._current_cores):
            raise AssertionError(
                "The number of cores to swap in is not the same as the number "
                "already swapped in. This is not allowed."
            )

        current_core_simobj = [
            core.get_simobject() for core in self._current_cores
        ]
        to_switch_simobj = [core.get_simobject() for core in to_switch]

        # Switch the CPUs
        m5.switchCpus(
            self._board, list(zip(current_core_simobj, to_switch_simobj))
        )

        # Ensure the current processor is updated.
        self._current_cores = to_switch

    def _pre_instantiate(self, root: Root) -> None:
        super()._pre_instantiate(root)
        # The following is a bit of a hack. If a simulation is to use a KVM
        # core then the `sim_quantum` value must be set. However, in the
        # case of using a SwitchableProcessor the KVM cores may be
        # switched out and therefore not accessible via `get_cores()`.
        # This is the reason for the `isinstance` check.
        #
        # We cannot set the `sim_quantum` value in every simulation as
        # setting it causes the scheduling of exits to be off by the
        # `sim_quantum` value (something necessary if we are using KVM
        # cores). Ergo we only set the value of KVM cores are present.
        #
        # There is still a bug here in that if the user is switching to and
        # from KVM and non-KVM cores via the SwitchableProcessor then the
        # scheduling of exits for the non-KVM cores will be incorrect. This
        # will be fixed at a later date.
        if self._prepare_kvm:
            m5.ticks.fixGlobalFrequency()
            root.sim_quantum = m5.ticks.fromSeconds(0.001)

from typing import Optional

from m5.util import warn

from ...isas import ISA
from ...utils.override import *
from ..boards.abstract_board import AbstractBoard
from ..boards.mem_mode import MemMode
from ..processors.cpu_types import (
    CPUTypes,
    get_mem_mode,
)
from ..processors.simple_core import SimpleCore
from .abstract_processor import AbstractProcessor
from .switchable_processor import SwitchableProcessor


class SimpleSwitchableProcessor(SwitchableProcessor):
    """
    A Simplified implementation of SwitchableProcessor where there is one
    processor at the start of the simuation, and another that can be switched
    to via the "switch" function later in the simulation. This is good for
    fast/detailed CPU setups.
    """

    def __init__(
        self,
        starting_core_type: CPUTypes,
        switch_core_type: CPUTypes,
        num_cores: int,
        isa: ISA = None,
    ) -> None:
        """
        :param starting_core_type: The CPU type for each type in the processor
                                   to start with (i.e., when the simulation has
                                   just started).
        :param switch_core_types: The CPU type for each core, to be switched
        to.

        :param isa: The ISA of the processor.
        """

        if num_cores <= 0:
            raise AssertionError("Number of cores must be a positive integer!")

        self._start_key = "start"
        self._switch_key = "switch"
        self._current_is_start = True

        self._mem_mode = get_mem_mode(starting_core_type)

        switchable_cores = {
            self._start_key: [
                SimpleCore(cpu_type=starting_core_type, core_id=i, isa=isa)
                for i in range(num_cores)
            ],
            self._switch_key: [
                SimpleCore(cpu_type=switch_core_type, core_id=i, isa=isa)
                for i in range(num_cores)
            ],
        }

        super().__init__(
            switchable_cores=switchable_cores, starting_cores=self._start_key
        )

    @overrides(SwitchableProcessor)
    def incorporate_processor(self, board: AbstractBoard) -> None:
        super().incorporate_processor(board=board)

        if (
            board.get_cache_hierarchy().is_ruby()
            and self._mem_mode == MemMode.ATOMIC
        ):
            warn(
                "Using an atomic core with Ruby will result in "
                "'atomic_noncaching' memory mode. This will skip caching "
                "completely."
            )
            self._mem_mode = MemMode.ATOMIC_NONCACHING
        board.set_mem_mode(self._mem_mode)

    @overrides(AbstractProcessor)
    def switch(self):
        """Switches to the "switched out" cores."""
        if self._current_is_start:
            self.switch_to_processor(self._switch_key)
        else:
            self.switch_to_processor(self._start_key)

        self._current_is_start = not self._current_is_start

Here is the complete picture of how gem5 CPU switching works.

---

## The Two Layers

### Python API: `m5.switchCpus()` (`src/python/m5/simulate.py:430`)

```python
m5.switchCpus(system, [(old_cpu, new_cpu), ...])
```

Internally does 4 things in sequence:

1. **`drain()`** - flush all in-flight memory ops, pipeline stages, etc.
2. **`old_cpu.switchOut()`** - quiesce the old CPU, flush TLBs, go to power-off state
3. **`_changeMemoryMode(system, mode)`** - switch atomic/timing/atomic_noncaching as required by the new CPU type (also flushes/invalidates caches if going to noncaching mode)
4. **`new_cpu.takeOverFrom(old_cpu)`** - the actual handoff (see C++ below)

### C++ handoff: `BaseCPU::takeOverFrom()` (`src/cpu/base.cc:634`)

Copies all state from old CPU to new CPU:
- Thread context (registers, PC) - via `newTC->takeOverFrom(oldTC)`
- MMU/TLB - via `newTC->getMMUPtr()->takeOverFrom(oldTC->getMMUPtr())`
- Interrupt lines - direct copy, old cleared
- **Port rebinding** - `getInstPort().takeOverFrom(&oldCPU->getInstPort())` and `getDataPort().takeOverFrom(...)` - reconnects the new CPU to the cache hierarchy

---

## Setup Requirements (before `m5.instantiate()`)

The standby CPU **must** be created with `switched_out=True` and must share `workload`, `clk_domain`, `isa` with the running CPU:

```python
switch_cpu = O3CPU(switched_out=True, cpu_id=0)
switch_cpu.workload  = testsys.cpu[0].workload   # same process
switch_cpu.clk_domain = testsys.cpu[0].clk_domain
switch_cpu.isa       = testsys.cpu[0].isa
switch_cpu.createThreads()
testsys.switch_cpus  = [switch_cpu]              # must be child of system
```

---

## Three Common Patterns

### Pattern 1: Fast-forward + Detailed (`--fast-forward N`)
`configs/common/Simulation.py:524-555`

```
AtomicSimpleCPU  ──(N insts)──►  O3CPU
    max_insts fires exit          takeOverFrom
```
```python
testsys.cpu[0].max_insts_any_thread = N   # triggers exit event
exit_event = m5.simulate()                # runs until exit
m5.switchCpus(testsys, switch_cpu_list)   # hand off to O3
m5.simulate()                             # detailed ROI
```

### Pattern 2: Three-phase (`--standard-switch`)
`configs/common/Simulation.py:595-651`

```
AtomicSimpleCPU ──(1 inst)──► TimingSimpleCPU ──(warmup)──► O3CPU
   checkpoint restore           cache warmup                  ROI
```

### Pattern 3: Modern stdlib (`SimpleSwitchableProcessor`)
`src/python/gem5/components/processors/simple_switchable_processor.py`

```python
processor = SimpleSwitchableProcessor(
    starting_core_type=CPUTypes.ATOMIC,
    switch_core_type=CPUTypes.O3,
    num_cores=1,
    isa=ISA.RISCV,
)
# In simulation exit handler:
processor.switch()   # calls m5.switchCpus internally
```

---

## Key Constraint

`new_cpu.support_take_over()` must return `True`. `KvmCPU`, `AtomicSimpleCPU`, `TimingSimpleCPU`, `O3CPU`, and `MinorCPU` all support it. The check at `simulate.py:469` enforces this before the switch.

from enum import Enum


class ExitEvent(Enum):
    """
    An enum class holding all the supported simulator exit events.

    The simulator will exit in certain conditions. The simulate package has
    been designed to categorize these into sensible states of exit, listed
    below.
    """

    EXIT = "exit"  # A standard vanilla exit.
    WORKBEGIN = "workbegin"  # An exit because a ROI has been reached.
    WORKEND = "workend"  # An exit because a ROI has ended.
    SPATTER_EXIT = "spatter exit"  # An exit because a spatter core has ended.
    SWITCHCPU = "switchcpu"  # An exit needed to switch CPU cores.
    FAIL = "fail"  # An exit because the simulation has failed.
    CHECKPOINT = "checkpoint"  # An exit to load a checkpoint.
    SCHEDULED_TICK = "scheduled tick exit"
    MAX_TICK = "max tick"  # An exit due to a maximum tick value being met.
    USER_INTERRUPT = (  # An exit due to a user interrupt (e.g., cntr + c)
        "user interupt"
    )
    SIMPOINT_BEGIN = "simpoint begins"
    MAX_INSTS = "number of instructions reached"
    PERF_COUNTER_ENABLE = "performance counter enabled"
    PERF_COUNTER_DISABLE = "performance counter disabled"
    PERF_COUNTER_RESET = "performance counter reset"
    PERF_COUNTER_INTERRUPT = "performance counter interrupt"
    KERNEL_PANIC = "kernel panic in simulated system"
    KERNEL_OOPS = "kernel oops in simulated system"

    @classmethod
    def translate_exit_status(cls, exit_string: str) -> "ExitEvent":
        """
        This function will translate common exit strings to their correct
        ExitEvent categorization.

        .. note::

            At present, we do not guarantee this list is complete, as
            there are no bounds on what string may be returned by the simulator
            given an exit event.
        """

        if exit_string == "m5_workbegin instruction encountered":
            return ExitEvent.WORKBEGIN
        elif exit_string == "workbegin":
            return ExitEvent.WORKBEGIN
        elif exit_string == "m5_workend instruction encountered":
            return ExitEvent.WORKEND
        elif exit_string == "workend":
            return ExitEvent.WORKEND
        elif exit_string == "m5_exit instruction encountered":
            return ExitEvent.EXIT
        elif exit_string == "exiting with last active thread context":
            return ExitEvent.EXIT
        elif exit_string == "simulate() limit reached":
            return ExitEvent.MAX_TICK
        elif exit_string == "Tick exit reached":
            return ExitEvent.SCHEDULED_TICK
        elif exit_string == "switchcpu":
            return ExitEvent.SWITCHCPU
        elif exit_string == "m5_fail instruction encountered":
            return ExitEvent.FAIL
        elif exit_string == "checkpoint":
            return ExitEvent.CHECKPOINT
        elif exit_string == "user interrupt received":
            return ExitEvent.USER_INTERRUPT
        elif exit_string == "simpoint starting point found":
            return ExitEvent.SIMPOINT_BEGIN
        elif exit_string == "a thread reached the max instruction count":
            return ExitEvent.MAX_INSTS
        elif exit_string == "performance counter enabled":
            return ExitEvent.PERF_COUNTER_ENABLE
        elif exit_string == "performance counter disabled":
            return ExitEvent.PERF_COUNTER_DISABLE
        elif exit_string == "performance counter reset":
            return ExitEvent.PERF_COUNTER_RESET
        elif exit_string == "performance counter interrupt":
            return ExitEvent.PERF_COUNTER_INTERRUPT
        elif exit_string == "Kernel panic in simulated system.":
            return ExitEvent.KERNEL_PANIC
        elif exit_string == "Kernel oops in simulated system.":
            return ExitEvent.KERNEL_OOPS
        elif exit_string.endswith("will terminate the simulation.\n"):
            # This is for the traffic generator exit event
            return ExitEvent.EXIT
        elif exit_string.endswith("is finished updating the memory.\n"):
            # This is for the gups generator exit event
            return ExitEvent.EXIT
        elif exit_string.endswith("received all expected responses."):
            return ExitEvent.SPATTER_EXIT
        raise NotImplementedError(
            f"Exit event '{exit_string}' not implemented"
        )

content.text:
Here are all the approaches to trigger a CPU switch in gem5:

---

## Approaches to Trigger CPU Model Switch

### 1. `max_insts_any_thread` (instruction count fast-forward)
Set a limit on the fast-forward CPU; when it fires, the exit event causes the switch.
```python
cpu[i].max_insts_any_thread = N   # fires "a thread reached the max instruction count"
exit_event = m5.simulate()
m5.switchCpus(system, [(cpu[i], switch_cpu[i])])
```
CLI: `--fast-forward N`

---

### 2. m5ops: `m5_workbegin` / `m5_workend` markers
Insert pseudo-instructions in the workload binary. The simulator exits on each annotation and the script switches CPUs on the `WORKBEGIN` event.
```c
// in workload:
m5_work_begin(0, 0);   // triggers ExitEvent.WORKBEGIN
// ... ROI ...
m5_work_end(0, 0);     // triggers ExitEvent.WORKEND
```
```python
on_exit_event={ExitEvent.WORKBEGIN: switch_cpu_generator}
```
This is the primary approach for annotated workloads (SPEC, etc.).

---

### 3. m5ops: `m5_switchcpu`
A dedicated pseudo-instruction specifically for CPU switching.
```c
m5_switch_cpu();   // triggers exit string "switchcpu" -> ExitEvent.SWITCHCPU
```
The stdlib's `Simulator` handles `ExitEvent.SWITCHCPU` by calling `processor.switch()`.

---

### 4. Checkpoint restore + switch (`--checkpoint-restore`)
Take a checkpoint with the fast CPU, restore it, then immediately switch to the detailed CPU for the ROI. The switch happens after restore with minimal fast-forward.
```
AtomicCPU --take-checkpoint--> [cpt] --restore-with-cpu AtomicCPU --> switch to O3
```
CLI: `--checkpoint-restore N --cpu-type O3CPU`

---

### 5. SimPoint-driven switching
Simpoints identify representative intervals. The CPU fires on `simpoint_start_insts` list entries, triggering exit `"simpoint starting point found"` (`ExitEvent.SIMPOINT_BEGIN`), then the script switches.
```python
cpu[0].simpoint_start_insts = [warmup_length, warmup_length + interval_length]
```
CLI: `--simpoint`, `--take-simpoint-checkpoints`, `--restore-simpoint-checkpoint`

---

### 6. `--standard-switch` (3-phase: Atomic -> Timing -> O3)
```
AtomicSimpleCPU --(1 inst or fast-forward)--> TimingSimpleCPU --(warmup insts)--> O3CPU
```
First switch warms up caches in timing mode, second switch enters detailed OoO simulation. CLI: `--standard-switch N --warmup-insts M`

---

### 7. Tick / time limit (`simulate(max_tick)`)
Call `m5.simulate(N_ticks)` for the fast-forward phase; when the tick limit fires (`ExitEvent.MAX_TICK`), switch CPUs.
```python
m5.simulate(10000)   # fast-forward for N ticks
m5.switchCpus(system, switch_cpu_list)
```

---

### 8. KVM + switch on exit
Use `KvmCPU` (host virtualization) for near-native fast-forward, then switch to O3 for detailed timing. The switch is triggered by any of the above events (usually `m5_workbegin` or instruction count).
```python
SimpleSwitchableProcessor(
    starting_core_type=CPUTypes.KVM,
    switch_core_type=CPUTypes.O3,
    ...
)
```
Requires: Linux host, KVM module, x86 ISA.

---

### Summary table

| Approach | Trigger source | Granularity | Annotation needed |
|---|---|---|---|
| `max_insts_any_thread` | CPU hardware counter | instruction count | No |
| `m5_workbegin`/`m5_workend` | binary pseudo-inst | exact ROI boundary | Yes (recompile) |
| `m5_switchcpu` | binary pseudo-inst | exact point | Yes (recompile) |
| Checkpoint restore | saved state | instruction boundary | No |
| SimPoint | profile-driven | interval-based | No (offline profile) |
| `--standard-switch` | instruction count | 3-phase | No |
| Tick limit | wall-clock sim time | tick granularity | No |
| KVM fast-forward | any of the above | native speed | Optional |

content.text:
The key difference is **purpose and conditionality**.

---

### `m5_switch_cpu`
`src/sim/pseudo_inst.cc:471`

```c
void switchcpu(ThreadContext *tc) {
    exitSimLoop("switchcpu");   // unconditionally exit, always
}
```

- No parameters. No conditions. No counters.
- Always fires `exitSimLoop("switchcpu")` every time it is executed.
- Single purpose: tell the script "switch the CPU now."

---

### `m5_work_begin(workid, threadid)` / `m5_work_end(workid, threadid)`
`src/sim/pseudo_inst.cc:497`

```c
void workbegin(ThreadContext *tc, uint64_t workid, uint64_t threadid) {
    if (params.exit_on_work_items)       // exit only if flag set
        exitSimLoop("workbegin", workid);
    // otherwise: check counters, maybe checkpoint, maybe exit, maybe nothing
    if (systemWorkBeginCount == params.work_begin_ckpt_count)
        exitSimLoop("checkpoint");
    if (systemWorkBeginCount == params.work_begin_exit_count)
        exitSimLoop("work started count reach");
    if (cpuId == params.work_begin_cpu_id_exit)
        exitSimLoop("work started on specific cpu");
    // if none of the above fire: simulation CONTINUES, no exit at all
}
```

- Has `workid` + `threadid` to identify which work item.
- Conditionally exits based on System params - may do nothing, checkpoint, or exit.
- Designed for multi-purpose ROI demarcation: stats dumps, checkpoints, exits, counted triggers.

---

### The real difference

| | `m5_switch_cpu` | `m5_work_begin/end` |
|---|---|---|
| Always exits sim? | Yes, unconditionally | No, depends on System params |
| Parameters | None | workid, threadid |
| Can take checkpoint? | No | Yes |
| Semantic meaning | "switch CPU now" | "ROI boundary N started/ended" |
| Exit string | `"switchcpu"` | `"workbegin"` / `"workend"` |
| Multiple work items? | No | Yes (via workid) |

---

### In practice

`m5_work_begin/end` is meant for **annotating what the workload is doing** - the simulation script decides what action to take on each boundary (switch CPU, dump stats, checkpoint, or nothing).

`m5_switch_cpu` is an **imperative command** embedded in the binary - it always forces a switch with no conditions.

For a standard fast-forward ROI flow, `m5_work_begin` is idiomatic because the same binary annotation works for multiple purposes (stats, checkpointing, CPU switch) just by changing System params or the exit handler - no binary recompile needed.

Here is the full breakdown.

---

## `--standard-switch N` - Three-Phase Pipeline

### CPU objects created (all 3 exist from startup)

```
testsys.cpu[i]        = AtomicSimpleCPU   (running)
testsys.switch_cpus[i]   = TimingSimpleCPU  (switched_out=True)
testsys.switch_cpus_1[i] = DerivO3CPU       (switched_out=True)
```

This is hardcoded at `Simulation.py:596-601` - you cannot choose the intermediate CPU type.

---

### Phase 1: AtomicSimpleCPU - fast-forward

The exit trigger for the first switch depends on flags combined with `--standard-switch`:

| Combined flag | `max_insts_any_thread` set to |
|---|---|
| `--checkpoint-restore` | 1 instruction (just enough to leave restore state) |
| `--fast-forward N` | N instructions |
| `--simpoint` | simpoint instruction count |
| none of the above | 1 instruction (switch almost immediately) |

```python
exit_event = m5.simulate()          # runs until max_insts fires
m5.switchCpus(testsys, switch_cpu_list)   # Atomic -> Timing
```

---

### Phase 2: TimingSimpleCPU - cache warmup

The exit trigger for the second switch is controlled by which flag you use:

| Flag | Warmup ends when |
|---|---|
| `--warmup-insts W` | W instructions executed (instruction-based) |
| only `--standard-switch N` | N **ticks** elapsed (tick-based, N is the integer you passed) |

```python
if options.warmup_insts:
    exit_event = m5.simulate()              # exits on max_insts
else:
    exit_event = m5.simulate(options.standard_switch)  # exits after N ticks
m5.switchCpus(testsys, switch_cpu_list1)    # Timing -> O3
```

So `N` in `--standard-switch N` is a **tick count for the warmup phase**, not an instruction count. That is the non-obvious part.

---

### Phase 3: DerivO3CPU - ROI

Runs until program exits or `--maxinsts M` fires on O3CPU.

---

### Why three phases instead of two?

**AtomicSimpleCPU does not model cache timing.** Its memory accesses bypass detailed cache state (atomic mode = no latency, no realistic fill/evict). After fast-forwarding with Atomic, caches are cold.

If you switch directly Atomic -> O3, the first N instructions in O3 suffer artificial cold-start misses that would not happen in reality, corrupting your measurements.

**TimingSimpleCPU uses real timing memory accesses**, so it fills the cache hierarchy (L1I, L1D, L2, ...) realistically before O3 takes over. By the time O3 starts, caches reflect the working set of the program at the ROI boundary.

```
Atomic (fast, cold cache) -> Timing (slow, warms cache) -> O3 (accurate, warm cache)
```

---

### Example invocation

```sh
gem5 configs/example/se.py \
  --cpu-type=DerivO3CPU \
  --fast-forward=1000000000 \   # Phase 1: 1B insts with Atomic
  --standard-switch=200000000 \ # Phase 2: warmup for 200M ticks with Timing
  --warmup-insts=5000000 \      # (overrides tick limit: 5M insts instead)
  --maxinsts=100000000 \        # Phase 3: measure 100M insts with O3
  --caches -c my_binary
```

From the code already read at `Simulation.py:631-636, 763-767`:

---

`--standard-switch N` - N is a **tick count** passed directly to `m5.simulate(N)` as the time limit for Phase 2 (TimingSimpleCPU warmup). The warmup ends after N simulated ticks elapse.

`--warmup-insts W` - sets `switch_cpus[i].max_insts_any_thread = W`, so Phase 2 ends after W **retired instructions** instead.

---

### How they interact

```python
# Simulation.py:763-767
if options.warmup_insts:
    exit_event = m5.simulate()              # instruction-based exit, N ignored
else:
    exit_event = m5.simulate(options.standard_switch)  # tick-based exit
```

| Flags used | Phase 2 ends when |
|---|---|
| `--standard-switch N` only | N ticks elapsed |
| `--standard-switch N --warmup-insts W` | W instructions retired, N is ignored |

---

### Which to use

**`--warmup-insts W`** is usually preferred - instruction count is portable across host speeds and gives a deterministic, reproducible warmup length regardless of clock frequency or simulation rate.

**`--standard-switch N` alone (tick-based)** is less predictable because the number of instructions executed in N ticks depends on IPC of TimingSimpleCPU and the workload. It is mainly there as a fallback when you have no good instruction count estimate.
